findings.message: This adds a new public `BranchReaderPort` implementation on `SystemGitRepo`, but there are no tests in `git_cli/mod.rs` covering the happy path or the error mapping for this adapter. That leaves the new public API unverified and makes regressions in the delegation/mapping easy to miss.
This patch is no longer limited to the single impl block requested in CN-05: it appends unit tests under `mod tests`, so the change surface extends beyond the allowed block.

The new `BranchReaderPort` adapter has no test for the only error branch (`GitError` -> `BranchReadError::ReadFailed`). The added tests cover the success path and the `Ok(None)` path only, so an error-mapping regression would not be caught.

This patch exceeds the briefing's CN-05 boundary: it adds a new `#[cfg(test)]` test block after the adapter impl, even though T002 was supposed to stay within a single `impl BranchReaderPort for SystemGitRepo` block in `libs/infrastructure/src/git_cli/` only.

`branch_reader_port_returns_none_for_empty_repo_with_no_commits` is built on the wrong Git assumption: `git init` creates an unborn branch with a known current branch name, so this path is not a reliable `None` case and is likely to fail in CI. Use a scenario where `current_branch` באמת cannot resolve a branch name instead.

The new error path tells users to "provide an explicit track-id", but `verify catalogue-spec-signals` has no track-id argument. On a non-track branch this remediation is impossible within this command, so the failure message contradicts the CLI surface.

On non-track branches this error still includes the shared resolver text `provide an explicit track-id` via `{e}`, but `verify catalogue-spec-signals` exposes no `--track` or `--track-id` argument. The remediation is impossible for this command, so the failure message contradicts the CLI surface; suppress or map that nested resolver detail here and keep the actionable hint to switch to a `track/<id>` branch.

`SystemGitRepo::discover_from(&project_root)` is run before `TaskOperationInteractor::new` here, so every `skip_branch_check = false` add/set/clear mutation now requires a git repository even when the interactor would have skipped the branch guard for a branchless track. That regresses valid branchless operations and also changes validation precedence (for example, a bad `task_id` can now surface as a git-discovery error if the repo is missing). The same eager-discovery pattern repeats at lines 84 and 125.

The same eager `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(&project_root)` happens before `TaskOperationInteractor` is invoked here, so transition now depends on git discovery even in cases where the use case would have rejected bad input first or skipped the branch guard for a branchless track. That breaks valid non-git/branchless transition flows and changes error precedence.

`resolve_track_id()` always calls `SystemGitRepo::discover()` on the process CWD. That is fine for the plain `track/*` commands, but it makes the new omitted-`track-id` path inconsistent for the `--workspace-root` commands in this file (`type-signals`, `baseline-graph`, `contract-map`, `catalogue-spec-signals`, `catalogue-impl-signals`, `baseline-capture`): if the CLI is launched from a different checkout or subdirectory, the helper can resolve a different active track than the workspace the command actually operates on, so the later workspace-root branch guards can fail or target the wrong track.

The added `resolve_track_id` tests short-circuit on repo discovery/current-branch failures and on non-matching branch shapes, so on detached HEAD, main, or any non-git CI environment they can all pass without exercising either the active-branch default or the fail-closed path. That leaves the new behavior required by the brief effectively unverified.

When `--track` is omitted, this resolves the active track from the process CWD via `resolve_track_id(args.track)`, but `CatalogueSpecRefsArgs` already carries `workspace_root`. If the command is invoked from a different checkout or wrapper, it can resolve the wrong track (or fail spuriously) instead of using the workspace being verified. Anchor branch discovery to `workspace_root` here.

An explicit `--track-id` still goes through the `Some(id) => Some(id)` fast path and is passed straight to `sync_rendered_views`, which joins it into `track/items/<id>` without validation. A malformed id like `../../tmp` can escape the intended directory and write outside the workspace. Validate the explicit id before calling `sync_rendered_views`.

`dispatch_track_commit_message` now treats non-track branches, detached HEAD, and branch-lookup failures as `Ok(None)` via `current_branch_track_id_strict()`, so the `review check-approved` gate is skipped entirely unless a `track/<id>` branch is detected. That is a regression from the previous fail-closed behavior and lets `cargo make track-commit-message` succeed on `main`/detached HEAD without running the approval check or persisting `.commit_hash`.

`--cleanup` now deletes `commit-message.txt` when the auto-detected branch guard fails. That is a behavior regression from the previous contract: recoverable guard failures should leave the message file in place so the operator can fix the branch/metadata issue and retry without recreating the commit message.

`commit_from_file` now deletes `tmp/track-commit/commit-message.txt` when the branch guard fails under `--cleanup`. The commit workflow documents cleanup of this scratch file on successful commit, and the previous guard-error paths left the commit message available for retry. A branch metadata mismatch or missing track claim now fails before `git commit` but still removes the prepared commit message, which is a behavioral regression in the guarded commit path.

`commit_from_file` now runs the non-track-branch fail-closed check only when `--track-dir` is absent. For an explicit track directory whose metadata has `branch: null` (the planning-only shape still accepted by `load_explicit_track_branch`), `verify_explicit_track_branch` returns `Ok(())`, so `sotp git commit-from-file ... --track-dir track/items/<id>` can still commit from `main`, another non-track branch, or detached/unresolvable branch states instead of rejecting. That weakens the required fail-closed non-track-branch rejection while preserving a planning-only commit path via the retained explicit flag.

`commit_from_file` no longer rejects every non-`track/` branch before applying an explicit selector. When `--track-dir` points at metadata with a non-null `branch`, `explicit_pins_branch` skips the `branch.starts_with("track/")` check, and `verify_explicit_track_branch` only checks equality. A track metadata value such as `"main"` would therefore allow committing on `main`, despite the T011 requirement that detached, non-track, and unresolvable branches fail closed.

T006 conflates the branch-resolution path for `verify-plan-artifact-refs-local` with the `track-id` optionality work in IN-01/IN-02/IN-03. That task is driven by the `--track-dir` flow in IN-09, so describing it as something the IN-01/02/03 changes will resolve is a semantic error and points the implementation at the wrong resolver.

T006 asks the Makefile tasks to both preserve `[SKIP]` / ‘skip gracefully’ behavior on non-track branches and also propagate a non-zero exit code with fail-closed semantics. Those requirements conflict, so the task cannot be implemented as written without choosing one behavior and dropping the other.

T005 treats TypeSignals, TypeGraph, BaselineGraph, ContractMap, CatalogueSpecSignals, SpecElementHash, BaselineCapture, and CatalogueImplSignals as flag `--track-id` commands, while spec IN-01 classifies those same commands as positional `track_id` commands and task coverage maps IN-01 only to T004. This leaves the spec, coverage, and implementation plan disagreeing about which task/spec element owns those CLI changes.

IN-10 requires adding tests for three cases, including explicit track-id priority, but T001 only adds resolver tests for track branch, non-track branch, and detached HEAD. T007 merely verifies acceptance criteria as the final gate, so no implementation task actually adds the explicit-track-id-priority test required by the spec.

T005 pulls `track views sync` into the shared resolver replacement and then says to fail closed on non-track branches “in all cases”. That conflicts with the ADR/spec intent for commands with meaningful no-track behavior: `track views sync` has a registry-only mode when no active track is present, so forcing fail-closed here would break that path instead of documenting/preserving the exception.

T005 says it covers all remaining flag-based commands but only lists `review codex-local`, `review claude-local`, `review check-approved`, and `review results`. The current review command set also has track-targeted `review local`, `review files`, and `review classify` commands with required `--track-id`; following this plan would leave those commands mandatory and outside the shared active-track resolver path, contradicting GO-01/IN-02's intent to make track-targeting `--track-id` commands optional. Add these commands to T005 coverage or explicitly mark them out of scope with a rationale.

BranchReaderPort is documented to return `None` for detached HEAD, while AC-08 requires the detached-HEAD case to produce `DetachedHead` and T001 says the interactor delegates to `resolve_track_id_from_branch`. The existing parser treats `None` as the separate `NoBranch` case, so following this contract will either fail AC-08 or require an undocumented remapping. Specify whether the port should pass through `Some("HEAD")` for detached HEAD or the interactor should translate `None` to `DetachedHead`.

IN-06 requires removing direct `git rev-parse` reads from `track views sync`, make helpers, and the write-guard injection path, but the coverage map assigns IN-06 only to T002/T003. T002 just adds the adapter impl and T003 covers the write-guard path; neither mapped task owns the views-sync or make-helper portions of IN-06. Add the tasks that actually cover those surfaces, such as T005/T006, or add a task that explicitly owns the remaining IN-06 work.

T006 removes the branch-derived arguments for `verify-spec-states-current-local` and says to replace the block with a direct CLI call after that command's current-track lookup is consolidated, but the plan never assigns any task/spec item to make `verify spec-states` resolve the active track itself. IN-09 only covers `track resolve`, `track views sync`, and `verify plan-artifact-refs`, while IN-03 covers `verify catalogue-spec-refs`; nothing creates the no-argument current-track path needed for `verify-spec-states-current-local`. As written, the Makefile cleanup is not executable for that task without an unplanned CLI change.

CN-01 says any omitted track-id on a non-`track/<id>` branch must fail closed, but T005 explicitly preserves `track views sync` registry-only mode when no `--track-id` is provided and no active track is resolvable. Because IN-09 brings `track views sync` into this plan, the artifacts define conflicting behavior for that command; the spec needs to document the registry-only exception or the task needs to remove it.

CN-01 still states that any omitted track-id on a non-`track/<id>` branch must fail closed, but T005 explicitly preserves `track views sync` registry-only mode when no active track is resolvable. That leaves the plan internally contradictory: implementers cannot both enforce this blanket constraint and keep the documented registry-only exception. The spec needs to document that command-specific exception (and any affected acceptance text should align with it).

P1: D4 describes the review tail as `sotp review`, and the same command name is propagated into IN-11/T008, but the CLI review operation is subcommand-based (`sotp review local`, `codex-local`, etc.); there is no executable parent `sotp review` command. If the plan is implemented as written, `track-local-review` will call an invalid command and fail to record the review. Name the concrete review subcommand or wrapper tail consistently across ADR/spec/plan.

The `SystemGitRepo` row is marked blue in `infrastructure-types.md`, but its corresponding task T002 is still `todo` in `impl-plan.json`. Under the plan-artifacts policy, only actually implemented task types should turn blue, so this creates an internal status mismatch.

`SystemGitRepo` is marked blue here, but the corresponding implementation task `T002` is still `in_progress` in `impl-plan.json` and still unresolved in `plan.md`. That makes the type-signal overstate completion; it should stay yellow until the task is actually done.

The implementation-phase briefing constrains plan-artifact changes for this track to progression markers, implemented-task ③ Blue signal updates, and generated view re-renders. This patch adds an entirely new ADR, plus a README index entry for it, which introduces new design content during the frozen implementation phase rather than just recording T004 progress. That contradicts the stated plan-artifact change boundary; remove this ADR/index change from this scope or route it through its own planning review.

`ActiveTrackResolveInteractor.new` is rendered twice in the contract map even though `usecase-types.json` declares only one `new` method. The duplicate node also has duplicate edge/class entries later in the same Mermaid graph, so the regenerated view is not semantically consistent with the catalogue and overstates the constructor relationship.

Removing `tmp/track-commit/track-dir.txt` from `TRANSIENT_AUTOMATION_FILES` does not change behavior while `TRANSIENT_AUTOMATION_DIRS` still contains `tmp`. `validate_stage_path_entries` still rejects every `tmp/**` path, so the track-dir file remains transient/excluded and the intended cleanup is not actually realized.

Removing `tmp/track-commit/track-dir.txt` from `TRANSIENT_AUTOMATION_FILES` is ineffective while `TRANSIENT_AUTOMATION_DIRS` still contains `tmp`. `validate_stage_path_entries` still rejects every `tmp/**` path, so the track-dir file remains blocked and the intended cleanup never takes effect.

This bullet conflates plain and tuple struct shapes. In the codec, only plain shapes use `fields[].ty`; tuple shapes store `fields` as plain strings (`Vec<String>`) with no `ty` field. As written, the convention teaches an invalid schema shape for tuple structs.

`.claude/agents/type-designer.md:403` overstates tuple behavior. The codec and domain model support `StructShape::Tuple { has_stripped_fields: true }`, so tuple structs with private fields are not guaranteed to length-mismatch. The follow-on advice to avoid tuple shape for dependency-holding structs is therefore incorrect.

The new R9 bullet at [knowledge/conventions/type-designer-kind-selection.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/knowledge/conventions/type-designer-kind-selection.md#L205) says `kind.shape.fields[].ty` for both plain and tuple shapes, but `StructShapeDto::Tuple` stores tuple fields as bare strings, not `{ ty }` objects. That wording would send authors toward a nonexistent schema path for tuple newtypes and contradict the codec DTO.

This inline bad example still shows a struct as `kind: { "kind": "struct" }` with no nested `shape`. CN-02 makes `shape` mandatory for every struct kind, and the briefing explicitly requires every struct kind JSON example to use the new `struct` + `shape` wire format. Even in a bad design example, this now teaches a schema-invalid catalogue shape rather than only the intended typestate-design violation.

Removing `--track-id` from the Step 2 / Step 4 / Step 6 examples makes the documented flow depend on `resolve_track_id(None)`, which only accepts `track/<id>` branches. Step 0 still says the review target can be `track/<id>` or `plan/<id>`, so the new bare invocations now fail on the documented `plan/<id>` path. Either keep an explicit track-id override for plan branches or remove `plan/<id>` from the supported branch set.

The happy-path commands now omit `--track-id`, but the same document still says `/track:review` can run from either `track/<id>` or `plan/<id>` branches. The underlying active-track resolver accepts only `track/<id>` branches, so from a `plan/<id>` branch this bare `cargo make track-review-results` invocation fails instead of using the extracted `{track-id}`. Either remove the `plan/<id>` promise from the command contract or keep passing an explicit `--track-id` for that path.

The command still advertises `plan/<id>` branches as valid review targets, and this new Step 2 fallback explicitly tells the operator to pass `--track-id` on `plan/<id>`, but the later happy-path commands now omit it unconditionally. `sotp review local`, `review results`, and `check-approved` only auto-resolve from `track/<id>` branches, so following this document from a `plan/<id>` branch will get through Step 2 only to fail at Step 4 or Step 6. Either constrain `/track:review` to `track/<id>` branches or carry the same explicit `--track-id` fallback through the later invocations.
